content.text: For FastAPI + Claude, Pydantic should validate both the inbound API contract from your clients and the outbound contract your backend promises to return, while your Claude service separately maps those models to and from the Messages API shape. The goal is to make invalid requests fail early, keep route contracts stable, and avoid leaking raw Claude SDK objects or provider-specific quirks directly to your frontend.
FastAPI uses Pydantic models to validate request bodies, coerce types, and generate OpenAPI docs automatically. It can also validate and serialize responses through return type annotations or
, ensuring clients only see the fields and types you intend to expose.
In a Claude-backed backend, this matters because there are really
want these to be identical, because your public API should be simpler, safer, and more stable than the provider’s raw request format.
A robust FastAPI + Claude app usually has four validation layers:
with Pydantic models in your FastAPI route.
for business rules like tenant policy, prompt size, allowed tools, or model allowlists.
where your service maps validated app models into the Messages API format.
with Pydantic response models so clients receive normalized output.
This layered approach prevents “garbage in, garbage out” and keeps failures easier to debug.

The most common pattern is to define request schemas that closely resemble your feature contract rather than Claude’s entire request surface. For example, if your feature is a chat endpoint, your frontend might only need
, and maybe a small set of tuning parameters.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict
from typing import Literal

class UserMessageRequest(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    session_id: str | None = None
    message: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=20000)
    system: str | None = Field(default=None, max_length=10000)
    model: str = Field(default="claude-sonnet-4-6")
    max_tokens: int = Field(default=1024, ge=1, le=8192)
    temperature: float | None = Field(default=None, ge=0, le=1)
rejects unexpected fields instead of silently accepting them.
, and numeric bounds catch invalid input before it reaches Claude.
You validate the values that matter to your product contract, not every possible Messages API option.
This is often better than exposing raw Claude-style
arrays unless your consumers truly need that flexibility.
If you are building a general-purpose backend wrapper around Claude, then modeling the Messages API shape more directly makes sense. In that case, use Pydantic models for roles and content blocks.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ConfigDict
from typing import Literal

class TextBlock(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    type: Literal["text"] = "text"
    text: str = Field(min_length=1)

class ChatMessage(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    role: Literal["user", "assistant"]
    content: list[TextBlock]

class CreateMessageRequest(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    model: str
    max_tokens: int = Field(ge=1, le=8192)
    messages: list[ChatMessage] = Field(min_length=1)
    system: str | None = None
    temperature: float | None = Field(default=None, ge=0, le=1)
This aligns well with the documented Messages API structure, where
are core fields, and each message has a
Pydantic field validation handles basic shape and type constraints, but Claude integrations usually need higher-level rules too. Examples include:
Ensuring the prompt does not exceed your app’s internal size budget before tokenization.
This is where your backend becomes opinionated and product-safe, instead of just being a raw passthrough.
FastAPI automatically recognizes Pydantic model parameters as request bodies and validates them before your handler runs. That means the route stays very simple:
If validation fails, FastAPI returns a structured 422 error automatically, which is one of the biggest advantages of using Pydantic for your API boundary.

Response validation is just as important as request validation. Claude SDK responses contain many fields you may not want to expose directly, and raw content blocks can vary depending on streaming, tools, or provider changes.
A response model lets you define a stable contract:

from pydantic import BaseModel

class UsageResponse(BaseModel):
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int

class MessageResponse(BaseModel):
    id: str
    model: str
    text: str
    stop_reason: str | None
    usage: UsageResponse
Your service then transforms the Claude response into this normalized structure:

async def create_message(self, body: CreateMessageRequest) -> MessageResponse:
    response = await self.client.messages.create(
        model=body.model,
        max_tokens=body.max_tokens,
        system=body.system,
        messages=[m.model_dump() for m in body.messages],
        temperature=body.temperature,
    )

    text = "".join(
        block.text for block in response.content
        if getattr(block, "type", None) == "text"
    )

    return MessageResponse(
        id=response.id,
        model=response.model,
        text=text,
        stop_reason=response.stop_reason,
        usage=UsageResponse(
            input_tokens=response.usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=response.usage.output_tokens,
        ),
    )
This protects your clients from needing to understand Anthropic content block internals.

One reason response models matter in FastAPI is that they filter output to match the schema, so you do not accidentally expose extra fields or internal metadata. That is useful in Claude integrations because your internal service may carry:
Your public response model should expose only what the client truly needs, such as

When you want Claude to return machine-readable data instead of free text, use a dedicated Pydantic model for the expected output shape and validate Claude’s structured response against it. Anthropic tooling and ecosystem guidance increasingly emphasize schema-driven structured outputs, where Claude is asked to emit JSON matching a provided schema.

class TicketClassification(BaseModel):
    category: str
    priority: Literal["low", "medium", "high"]
    sentiment: Literal["negative", "neutral", "positive"]
    summary: str
Ask Claude for strict JSON matching that structure.
This is much safer than trusting arbitrary JSON text from the model without validation.
Separate public schemas from provider schemas
A very important design principle is to maintain different schema layers for:
: what your FastAPI endpoints accept and return.
: internal business objects used by your application.
This separation keeps the rest of your codebase insulated from Messages API churn and lets you redesign your product API without rewriting your Claude integration every time.
For Claude-backed routes, these Pydantic checks are especially useful:
Maximum prompt lengths for user/system strings.
Allowed model enum or allowlist validator.
Valid role enums for message arrays.
Minimum one message in a conversation.
Tool names validated against a registry.
Metadata size and key restrictions if you expose metadata fields.
These catch many integration bugs before they become provider errors or wasted token spend.
Pydantic and FastAPI will automatically produce 422 validation errors for malformed requests. That means you should reserve your own custom exceptions for business-level failures like:
Conversation too large for plan tier.
Model not allowed for this tenant.
Claude returned invalid structured JSON after retries.
This cleanly separates “bad input shape” from “valid input, but rejected by business logic or downstream behavior.”
Strong Pydantic request and response models make tests much easier because they define the contract in executable code. You can test:
Claude structured output parsing fails correctly on malformed model output.
For example, a test can assert that an unexpected field is rejected when
is active, which protects your API from drift over time.
A clean FastAPI + Claude validation stack looks like this:
— converts request models into Messages API calls and normalizes outputs.
— declare Pydantic request bodies and response models only.

That pattern gives you a strict, explicit API contract at the FastAPI boundary and a separately controlled mapping layer for Claude’s Messages API, which is the safest and most maintainable way to do request/response validation with Pydantic.
